---
title: "Life Expectancy Predictor — WHO Data 2000–2015"
author: "Final Project"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: true
    transition: slide
---

## The Problem

Life expectancy differs by **more than 40 years** between countries.

- It is the single clearest summary of a nation's health — and of whether
  health policy is working.
- Governments and health organizations need to know **which levers actually
  move it**: vaccines? spending? education?

**What we're predicting:** given a country's health and economic indicators,

1. its **life expectancy in years**, and
2. whether it falls in the **Low, Medium, or High** group.

## Dataset & Approach {.smaller}

**The data:** World Health Organization records — **193 countries**, every year
from **2000 to 2015** (~2,900 country-years), covering immunization rates,
disease prevalence, schooling, income, and health spending.

**Two prediction tasks:**

- *"How long?"* — predict life expectancy in years
- *"Which group?"* — predict the Low / Medium / High band

**The honest test:** models were evaluated only on countries they had
**never seen during training** — no memorizing a country and getting quizzed
on its neighboring year.

## Key Findings — Regression

**How close can we get to a country's true life expectancy?**

| | Average error |
|---|---|
| Naive guess (global average) | **~7.3 years** |
| Our best model (XGBoost) | **~2.2 years** |

The model is more than **3× more accurate than guessing** — for countries
it has never seen.

## Key Findings — Classification

**Can we sort unseen countries into Low / Medium / High life expectancy?**

| | Correct |
|---|---|
| Naive guess (always pick the biggest group) | **34%** |
| Our best model (XGBoost) | **77%** |

The model also ranks countries by risk extremely well (ROC-AUC 0.92,
where 1.0 is perfect and 0.5 is coin-flipping). Most mistakes are
"one band off" — Low is almost never confused with High.

## What Drives Life Expectancy {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
1. **HIV/AIDS prevalence & adult mortality** — the strongest predictors.
   Partly by definition: where adults die young, life expectancy is low.
2. **Schooling & income composition** — the most interesting finding.
   Beyond direct mortality, how educated and economically developed a
   population is tells most of the rest of the story.
3. **Immunization coverage & health spending** — matter, but less than
   the above.
:::

. . .

> Education and income predict life expectancy better than most direct
> health-spending measures in this data.

## Limitations & Recommendations {.smaller}

**Limitations — what this model cannot do:**

- **Patterns, not causes** — it doesn't prove schooling *causes* longer lives.
- **Countries, not people** — national averages say nothing about individuals.
- **2000–2015, self-reported** — data is weakest exactly where health systems are.

**Recommendations:**

- Use it as a **screening tool**: flag countries whose indicators don't match
  their reported life expectancy, and investigate.
- Treat **education and income as health policy**, not side issues.

. . .

*Everything is reproducible — full report, live prediction app, and code in
the project repository.*
